hawkish_phrases: Inflation has been revised up for 2026, mainly because staff now expect services inflation to decline more slowly
Services inflation rose to 3.4 per cent in October and 3.5 per cent in November
Compensation per employee rose at an annual rate of 4.0 per cent. This was more than expected in the September staff projections
A slower reduction in wage pressures could delay the decline in services inflation
Market rates have increased since our last meeting
dovish_phrases: inflation should stabilise at the 2% target in the medium term
Staff expect it to stay below 2 per cent on average in 2026 and 2027
Inflation could turn out to be lower if the rise in US tariffs reduces demand for euro area exports
labour demand cooled further, with the job vacancy rate at its lowest level since the pandemic
An improvement in confidence could stimulate private spending
summary: The Governing Council held rates unchanged at 2.00% (deposit facility) with no vote split disclosed, maintaining a mildly hawkish stance due to upward inflation revisions for 2026, sticky services inflation at 3.5%, and wage growth exceeding expectations, despite guidance remaining data-dependent with no pre-commitment to a rate path.
